speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]. The second tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you consider, then, that you lived in a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: By no means, no. Our closest neighbor were non-Jews. And they had a daughter my age, and we were actually very close friends, and we were in each other's homes frequently. And my parents had neighbors, non-Jews. So they were their-- they were very good neighbors. No, we were not.

speaker: interviewer
text: So talk about the Jews who were not Orthodox.

speaker: interviewee
text: Los Angeles had three type of Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean Satu Mare.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: See how comfortable you are here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I am sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course Satu Mare. There were the Orthodox [? image ?] we were part of. I cannot tell you which segment was the larger one. And then there were Status Quo. I don't know. I guess you would compare it to Reform. And then there were Neolog. No, pardon me. The Status Quo would be like Conservative, and the Neolog would be like Reform. But the Status Quo had also a beautiful synagogue, which was-- what shall I tell you-- the-- the real [INAUDIBLE] Jews call a synagogue kosher, the one which is segregated properly. And the one which is not segregated properly is not such a kosher synagogue. So this was also-- because the women were upstairs, but you could see downstairs. It was a beautiful building, again, and the-- the chief rabbi was a doctor rabbiner. He had a doctorate in philosophy, a very imposing, elegant man, very intelligent and highly educated and secularly also. He was Dr. [? Klein. ?] And then was the Reform. I-- we had very little contact with that-- the other segments of the Jewish community. I had some because I went to [ROMANIAN] with some of the-- Status Quo, and I was in some of their homes. They were lovely people, but not observant like we were. And it was-- they lived a very nice social life. It was-- a lot among them, very well-to-do, with very elegant people, big businesses, big-name businesses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they fall under the aegis of the the Jewish administration that--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they had their own. They had their own. This was the Orthodox, and they had their own.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Now, let's talk about-- you were born in 1925. So at what point-- how old were you and what year was it-- when you began to realize that-- that the Germans, that there was a war raging and that it was going to impact on your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] how old? The war-- well, of course I was aware of the war from-- I read newspapers from a very, very young age. And we were always hoping-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a kind of hope that-- that the-- the-- the-- the Germans are going to-- are not going to succeed, you know? They-- we are-- they are going to win, and everything is going to be fine. Although it was, I guess, in 1942-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yes. In 1940-- let-- let me-- let me backtrack a little. When I went to [ROMANIAN] and I was in fifth grade, I-- then already the Germans were taking over-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] taking over Czechoslovakia and Europe. And of course, it reverberated in Romania also. They-- they brought in some laws, which were not-- not existing before, that in the high schools-- in the [ROMANIAN], which was the state school, they didn't allow only a certain percentage of Jews-- 6% for a couple of years and a couple of year later none. So of course, that touched my life already because I couldn't go on any further.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old would you have been at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: At this point, I-- I started school at seven, 11, I had to be about 14, 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this would have been, like, about '39 or '40--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that disrupted my life. I would have gone on to school, and I didn't know what to do. But it disrupted-- but the whole Jewish life was disrupted, not only my life personally. And-- but again, as I tell you, there were shortages. There were-- there were terrible rumors. There were trains going through the Satu Mare train station who brought Jews from Ukraine and from Russia who were already taken away from their homes. But we thought that this is Russia. This is communist. And these Jews were not-- they-- they explained that-- that they were not really citizens. They didn't have citizenship, and that's why they had to-- they were not safe. So they-- but we said, it cannot happen to us. I remember a Saturday afternoon when we walked to the train station, which was quite a walk, and we saw these cars filled with Jews, with Jews standing there. They stopped there. And we tried to-- we talked to them and tried to bring little things to help. But it did not occur-- it seemed so far, this cannot happen to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said that there were--

speaker: interviewee
text: We are-- we are-- we are not only citizens, but patriots. My father was such a Hungarian patriot that it was unbelievable. This-- this cannot happen with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you said that there, besides what happened in the school, there were other restrictions on Jews that were happening between '39, '40, '41. What other restrictions [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they started to take away certain businesses. And then as time went on, they took away more and more. Certain things what were-- what needed permission-- permits from the government, the-- the Jews were completely cut out. And of course, then, two years later we wore the yellow star.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you wear the yellow star? What time was-- what year was that? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: The yellow star we wore-- it was '44 when we were taken into the ghetto, so it was the year before.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So now, you had said that the-- that you had-- that they took away the businesses, and they didn't give permits. Who was they? Was it the Romanian authorities, or-- or were there Germans already involved in this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. It was done by the Romanians, but it was influenced by the Germans. And oh, pardon me, but in the mean-- while-- while I-- while I was still in-- in Satu Mare, Satu Mare became Hungary. It reverted back to Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Hungarians were very willing instruments in-- in-- in-- in executing and enforcing all the laws. They-- they-- they didn't have-- they were not heartbroken about the-- the German influence and laws and things. So it was done by the Hungarian [INAUDIBLE], not by the Romanians, because we were Hungary then again. How were the Romanians as people as far as being antisemitic? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Romanians-- when I went to school, I did not feel antisemitism. I knew that I cannot do certain things, but it did not occur to me that I should have wanted do it. And as I told you, I had Romanian friends. And-- and one very good thing was the Romania, you could do business. For money, you could do everything. So the Jews and the Romanians lived very well together. It was very different in Hungary and-- and after the Hungarians came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when was it that-- that you were given the stars to wear? Do you remember that? Do you remember how they said, you will all wear stars-- the yellow stars now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was a young girl. Of course, I remember when we started. But how it happened exactly, it was-- it-- it was-- we were devastated. We-- we couldn't go out only certain hours. And-- but Satu Mare, the stars and the whole ghetto period were short.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just ask you, besides--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything happened--

speaker: interviewer
text: --besides being devastated, were you outraged? Was there a sense of, how dare they, or-- or-- or not? Was there more resignation?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was more resignation. It was more resignation. You know, we did not live in a ghetto, but the Jews grew up with a ghetto mentality, that you-- you-- you accept it, and you had to comply. And that's-- that's how. That was the-- the-- the-- the-- the philosophy. That was the [? fate, ?] the mentality, that you don't-- revolution or something, uprising, I heard only in the-- in the Polish ghetto, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: That was nothing ever--

speaker: interviewer
text: --which later on--

speaker: interviewer
text: --to revolt or to-- to, in some way, resist what was happening to you just wasn't part of the mentality?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't. But again, I think that it's a fair explanation that they would-- we would have lived much longer under these circumstances, and-- and life would have been organized in some manner. Because our staying in the ghetto was not organized at all. It was a makeshift thing, you know? And there was no time, because we were a few weeks-- four, six weeks-- and that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's talk about the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so if we would have lived much longer, then maybe something like that. The whole thing was too short, and I guess we were-- we were in the shocking stage.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it happen that you-- that you were ordered into the ghetto? Do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember actually how it was ordered. I remember when we had to pack, and I still was a young girl. We had to pack just minimal belongings, close the door with furniture, with everything, and taken into another part of the city what we-- we frequented rarely, where they-- they-- they kind of cordoned off a few blocks and-- and pushed Jews into, I don't know how many families, in one house. Like, we lived in one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm, your whole family.

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it was-- but again, I tell you, it-- it's-- it's vague. It's a mish-mash. It's a mix up. It's-- it's terrible. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about-- do you remember was it all of the Jews from the town or just-- I mean, do you remember the-- like you called them the Status Quo or the-- the more Reform Jews? Did they also have to enter the-- the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody had to enter the ghetto. But there were three families in-- in Satu Mare who were exempt, who had-- whose parents had some very special-- how do you call when somebody has a medal--

speaker: interviewer
text: Honors, awards.

speaker: interviewee
text: Honors, awards from the Hungarian-- from the Austro-Hungarian government. I don't know, military service or what. And there, those three families were exempt. When-- when I came back, I found them, and they were not deported.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they lived through the whole war in the town.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in Satu Mare.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Satu Mare.

speaker: interviewee
text: But otherwise, everybody. But some people fled.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some people. I know also some. Even [NON-ENGLISH] Schwartz's sister, who, with-- with documents, which were not false documents, she fled. And she went-- she fled to Romania, because in Romania there was no deportation, not at that time, at least. So some fled, but those were rare individuals, you know. A family that was-- I-- I don't think I heard a whole family fleeing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, as far as the ghetto, you said it was a part of town you were not familiar with. Was it a Jewish part of town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You would call it more of a Jewish part than we lived in, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Apartments or houses or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, houses. Apartments were only in the-- you know, big buildings with several stories were only in the center of the city. Otherwise, it was private homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so they moved you in, and you-- and there were people living there. So what, they had to all move into one room, and you moved into another room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. They had to give over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was with you in the ghetto in your family? Which part-- which sisters and brothers, and your mother and father.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of them. Only one brother who was in-- in-- in the labor. And when-- when the Germans and the Hungarians came in, the Jews were not taken for military service, the Jewish young men. They were taken in labor camps. They worked behind the-- the front line doing cleaning up, you know, after bombing and after these kind of work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one of my brothers was-- was doing that, so he wasn't home. But the rest of us were in the ghetto, but not all my brothers and sisters because some were married, and they didn't live in Satu Mare. So they were-- Was it only Satu Mare, or were there adjacent areas that were also enclosed in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, there were adjacent area, different-- my husband, who lived about three hours away from [NON-ENGLISH], was taken into another town in a-- in-- in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know your husband at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: He was just-- I mean, he wasn't your husband. We have to make that clear.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he wasn't my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, as I mentioned before, my grandfather had a mill. And when he passed away, it was inherited by my uncle, by the oldest son. And then he took in a partner who was my husband's father. So I knew the family. And he-- so the people from [NON-ENGLISH] were taken to [NON-ENGLISH]. That was a smaller town than Satu Mare. But sure, there were many.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- but in the ghetto in Satu Mare, was it people from the neighboring, like, outlying towns were also forced into the ghetto, or was it predominantly town people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't believe so. I think it was only the Jews from Satu Mare.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you were in Satu Mare, and all of the Jews from the town are now enclosed in the ghetto. And what, do you know what's happening next? Do you know what is going to happen? Are you given any warning?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were rumors that we are going to be taken to-- to some places to work for the war effort.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So-- but there were all kind of rumors going around. We had no factual information. So they started to empty the ghetto at the end of June, and we were taken out of the ghetto by transports.

speaker: interviewer
text: By transports.

speaker: interviewee
text: Several-- several groups. It-- we-- it was called transport, several groups. And we were taken to the railway station.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- were you walked there or taken in cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: You walked.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, let me tell you something very-- that I'll never forget. It's in front of my eyes. We were taken in groups, as I mentioned, and we were-- we were-- we were walking in the middle of the street. Every traffic was stopped. The street was practically empty. The Gentiles were looking out through the closed window just behind the-- the-- the curtains out to the street. And we were marched a row of five and, of course, escorted by Hungarian police with-- with arms. And what-- and yes, let me backtrack again a little. Before we were taken into the ghetto, the Jews were ordered to shave their beards. The Orthodox [? form ?] Jews were wearing beards. And my father had a beard, and he had to shave his beard. Now, let me tell you. My father, as I mentioned, was quite a few years older than my mother. When I was born, I had already big brothers-- big-- big siblings from the first marriage. So my-- I remember my father as an older man always with a beard. And to see him without a beard-- he was gray already. I will never forget that. For days I was crying, and I couldn't look at him. He was white. He was wrinkled, you know, under the beard. It was a trauma that-- it was the first-- that most terrible experience. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were walking. We were taken to-- to the railway station-- [INAUDIBLE] go back, a-- a group-- when the-- when the-- when the ghetto was emptied. And my father, they're walking-- an old man, as I tell you. Suddenly, he became much older than he was. And as he passed our house-- Your [? own. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: --and he walking with his tears streaming down. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I will never forget that. We reached the station. It was quite a walk. And we were taken into boxcars, and we were separated from my mother. Because they took a certain amount of people until that boxcar filled up, and then the rest of the transport they put into another boxcar. And that's what happened with us. And that's when I saw my mother the last time, at the railway station in Satu Mare. And my father didn't want to board the-- the-- the-- the train, and they pushed him to, because he was screaming, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], my mother. And he sent me that I should look for her. But I couldn't-- I couldn't find her. So one sister and myself and my father went into another-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] another [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] car, in the railway car, and the rest of the family were in the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: With my mother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that, you know, I-- I didn't think of anything else, where we are going or what's going to happen. I was-- I just thinking that I'm separated from my mother, and my father is crying there. [INAUDIBLE]. The trip to Auschwitz, because that's where we were-- that's what the destination was-- of course, we didn't know where-- took two days and two nights. So we arrived in Auschwitz the day after-- after [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So that would have been June of-- of 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's where we keep the yahrzeit, the first day after [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Is the day that you keep as a yahrzeit for your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. For my parents and the rest of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, so I told you it was a trip of two days--

speaker: interviewer
text: Two days.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and two nights. In these boxcars, little windows which had iron rods on it. And I managed to get to the window, and there were peasants-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] peasants outside, you know, by the railway car.

speaker: interviewee
text: And instead that they should give us something, I ask them, where are we going? And they screamed back in Hungarian, to your death. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I took back-- I didn't tell anything to my father. But I thought that's antisemitism, you know. I didn't think that they know anything. But they were vicious like-- So we arrived in Auschwitz. Could you imagine? Tired, broken, confused [INAUDIBLE]. Hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And hungry, of course. But-- but the other feelings, as it overtook the hunger, the-- the despair, the-- the-- the-- and I-- they took us-- we arrived in Zittau. They opened the wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, you arrived in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived in Auschwitz. They opened the wagon, and they herded us out from the-- from the-- from the railway--

speaker: interviewer
text: Car.

speaker: interviewee
text: --car. And there stood German soldiers with their arms and with dogs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we schlepped our bundles. We were dressed in two, three-- three best dresses, hoping that-- that what we could take is going to remain. And we were-- and they were rushing us, and we couldn't take all the bundles with us, and we were upset. Who knew that what's coming that we wouldn't need the bundles. But I still wanted to-- to look for my mother. And I-- and I walked a few steps, and a soldier [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wrenched me back. And so I didn't see my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they took away my father, too, because Mengele was there. And suddenly we were just lined up, and we started to go march somewhere. And somebody's there, an officer with-- a tall, good-looking man dressed as an officer with those high boots and with a-- he had like a riding-- how do you call it? Crop. Like a whip.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, a whip. And he just moved the whip left and right, and my father was taken away. We were-- we were pushed to one side, and my father was away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it with your sister? You were with your sister then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was with one sister, and this is the only sister who remained-- she passed away two years ago in New York-- but who survived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Would you like me to continue on Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Let me ask you another question, though, before you do. Your mother was with your other siblings, and they were young like you were. And I know some of them were older.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some were.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Some were.

speaker: interviewer
text: --but they weren't very old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some were quite older, and some were younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't have any idea why it was that they did not survive at all? No idea?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. My younger sister was too young. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] An older sister passed away at home, pneumonia. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was before the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the ghetto. So my brothers-- I-- I didn't have other sisters. I had a sister who passed away, a younger sister who was too young-- you know, the children were also annihilated like-- like the-- like the parent-- and a sister who survived and myself. So we were four and five brothers. Two brothers did not live in Satu Mare. They were married. They had their own families. And the other three were taken. I had-- I never had word of them. Although no, somebody did come back and who said who was with one of my brothers. And he kind of knew an approximate date of when he disappeared, of his death. So that's the yahrzeit what we observe.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll go to another tape, and we'll talk about Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
